questions.question: Explain the term 'transaction' in the context of JDBC. What are its key characteristics?
questions.hint: Focus on the definition and key characteristics of database transactions
keyPoints: A transaction is a logical unit of work containing one or more database operations
Transactions ensure atomicity - all operations succeed or all fail
Transactions maintain data consistency and integrity
Multiple SQL statements can be grouped into a single transaction
Transactions can be committed (made permanent) or rolled back (undone)

questions.question: List and explain the four ACID properties of database transactions.
questions.hint: ACID is an acronym - remember each property and its significance
keyPoints: Atomicity: All operations complete successfully or none do
Consistency: Database moves from one valid state to another
Isolation: Concurrent transactions appear to execute serially
Durability: Once committed, changes persist even after system failure

questions.question: What is the purpose of setAutoCommit(false) in JDBC? When should it be used?
questions.hint: Think about scenarios where multiple SQL operations need to be treated as a single unit
keyPoints: setAutoCommit(false) disables automatic commit mode
All SQL statements are executed as part of a single transaction
It should be used when multiple database operations need to be grouped atomically
Developer has control over when to commit or rollback using commit() and rollback() methods
Default auto-commit mode commits each statement immediately

questions.question: Explain the difference between commit() and rollback() methods in JDBC with use cases.
questions.hint: Remember: commit saves changes, rollback reverts changes
keyPoints: commit() makes all changes permanent in the database since last commit/rollback
rollback() undoes all changes made since last commit/rollback
commit() is used when all operations succeed
rollback() is used when any operation fails or error occurs
Both end the current transaction and start a new one

questions.question: What are savepoints in JDBC? How do they improve transaction management?
questions.hint: Think of savepoints as checkpoints within a transaction
keyPoints: Savepoints are markers within a transaction that allow partial rollback
Created using Connection.setSavepoint() or setSavepoint(String name)
Allow rolling back to a specific point without losing entire transaction
Useful for complex transactions with multiple logical steps
Released using Connection.releaseSavepoint(savepoint)

questions.question: What happens if auto-commit is enabled (default) during a batch update operation?
questions.hint: Consider what happens when one statement in a batch fails with auto-commit ON
keyPoints: Each statement is committed individually after execution
If one statement fails, previous statements cannot be rolled back
No atomicity guarantee for the batch operation
May lead to partial updates and data inconsistency
Not suitable for dependent operations that must succeed together

questions.question: Explain in detail the transaction management process in JDBC with a complete code example showing proper error handling.
questions.hint: Include complete code with proper exception handling and resource cleanup
keyPoints: Load JDBC driver and establish connection
Disable auto-commit using setAutoCommit(false)
Create Statement/PreparedStatement objects
Execute SQL operations within try-catch block
Call commit() in try block after successful operations
Call rollback() in catch block on exception
Finally block to reset auto-commit to true and close connection
Use try-with-resources for automatic resource management

questions.question: Discuss the various transaction isolation levels in JDBC. Explain the concurrency problems each level addresses.
questions.hint: Remember the four standard isolation levels and their characteristics
keyPoints: TRANSACTION_READ_UNCOMMITTED: Allows dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads
TRANSACTION_READ_COMMITTED: Prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads
TRANSACTION_REPEATABLE_READ: Prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads
TRANSACTION_SERIALIZABLE: Prevents all three concurrency problems
Set using Connection.setTransactionIsolation(int level)
Higher isolation reduces concurrency but improves data integrity

questions.question: Design a JDBC program to perform a bank fund transfer from account A to account B, ensuring atomicity. Include proper error handling and rollback mechanism.
questions.hint: Consider edge cases like insufficient balance and network failures
keyPoints: Get connection and disable auto-commit
Write SQL to deduct amount from source account
Write SQL to add amount to destination account
Write transaction record for audit trail
Use try-catch-finally structure
Commit transaction only if all operations succeed
Rollback in catch block if any operation fails
Reset auto-commit in finally block
Handle insufficient balance scenario

questions.question: Compare local transactions with distributed transactions in JDBC. When would you use each type?
questions.hint: Think about scenarios requiring operations across multiple databases
keyPoints: Local transactions: Operate on single database, managed by that database
Distributed transactions: Span multiple databases or resources, require transaction manager
Local uses JDBC's standard transaction control (commit/rollback)
Distributed requires JTA (Java Transaction API) and XA protocol
Local is simpler with lower overhead
Distributed needed for ACID across multiple systems (e.g., two-phase commit)
XADataSource for distributed transaction enlistment

questions.question: Consider a scenario: Account A has Rs. 5000, Account B has Rs. 3000. A transfer of Rs. 2000 is initiated. Show the state of both accounts after each SQL operation if auto-commit is FALSE and the program crashes after the second UPDATE. Also explain if savepoints could help.
questions.hint: Trace the state changes step by step considering what happens at each point
keyPoints: Initial state: A=5000, B=3000
After first UPDATE (deduct): A=3000, B=3000
After second UPDATE (add): A=3000, B=5000
With rollback: Both revert to initial state
With savepoint after first update: A=3000, B=3000 remains
Savepoint allows partial rollback to known good state
Without transactions: A=3000, B=5000 - inconsistent state persists

questions.hint: Understand the difference between committed, uncommitted, and rolled-back states
keyPoints: If stops after step 6: First UPDATE executed, second UPDATE executed but not committed
Uncommitted changes not visible to other transactions in READ_COMMITTED or higher
On reconnection: Transaction is still active, needs rollback or commit
If step 7 fails: con.rollback(sp1) reverts to state after step 4
After rollback to sp1: First UPDATE remains (balance deducted from account 1)
Then can either fix error and retry or rollback entire transaction
Savepoint allows granular recovery without losing entire transaction work

questions.question:
An e-commerce system processes orders with the following operations:
- INSERT into orders table
- UPDATE inventory table (decrement stock)
- INSERT into payment table
- UPDATE customer loyalty points

Design the transaction logic assuming: (a) All operations must succeed, (b) Inventory update fails at step 2, (c) After rollback, the company wants to retry only the payment processing. Explain how savepoints help in scenario (c).
questions.hint: Analyze which operations are dependent and which can be independent
keyPoints: Set auto-commit false at start
Create savepoint after orders insert (sp1)
Create savepoint after inventory update (sp2)
On inventory failure at step 2: rollback() reverts all changes
With savepoint sp1 after orders: Can rollback to sp1
Retry payment processing from orders already committed
Savepoint allows partial success: orders placed, inventory unchanged
Trade-off: Must handle partial completion logically in application code
Better approach: Separate into independent transactions if logically valid

questions.question: What is a deadlock in database transactions? How does JDBC help in detecting and handling deadlock situations?
questions.hint: Deadlocks are handled at database level, not JDBC level directly
keyPoints: Deadlock occurs when two or more transactions wait indefinitely for each other to release locks
JDBC does not directly detect deadlocks - database handles this
Database typically uses timeout mechanism to detect deadlocks
SQLException may include SQL state indicating deadlock (SQLSTATE = '40001')
Application should implement retry logic on deadlock detection
Use shorter transactions and consistent access order to prevent deadlocks

questions.question: Explain the concept of transaction nesting in JDBC. Can JDBC truly support nested transactions? Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Distinguish between savepoints and true nested transactions
keyPoints: JDBC does NOT support true nested transactions
Savepoints provide appearance of nesting but not true nesting
setSavepoint() creates named savepoints within single transaction
Nested commits are not allowed - commit() commits entire transaction
Rollback to savepoint doesn't end the transaction
True nested transactions would allow inner commit independent of outer
Savepoints are the closest JDBC equivalent to nested transactions
Some frameworks layer true nesting on top of JDBC using separate connections
